content: Surrogate endpoint validation requires: strong biological plausibility linking surrogate to clinical outcome, epidemiological evidence showing surrogate predicts clinical benefit, and meta-analytic evidence demonstrating treatment effects on surrogate correlate with effects on clinical outcome (Prentice criteria). FDA acceptance varies by context: HbA1c is validated for diabetes drug approval; LDL cholesterol for cardiovascular risk reduction; tumor response/PFS accepted in many cancers but OS preferred when feasible. Limitations of surrogates include: may not capture all treatment effects (beneficial or harmful), validation is context-specific (may not generalize across drug classes), and historical examples where surrogate improvement didn't translate to clinical benefit (e.g., arrhythmia suppression with antiarrhythmics increased mortality). Patient-reported outcomes (PROs) increasingly valued as co-primary endpoints.
